input: What will the weather be like in Pune over the next 7 days? Should I irrigate my wheat field?
expected_output: Pune 7-day forecast: Days 1–2 partly cloudy, max 28–30°C, min 14–16°C, no rain. Day 3 light showers expected (3–5mm), max 26°C. Days 4–5 clear, max 29°C, min 15°C. Days 6–7 partly cloudy with a chance of thundershowers (8–12mm), max 27°C. Wind: 8–14 km/h from NW, gusts to 22 km/h on Day 7. Humidity 45–65%. Irrigation recommendation: With light showers on Day 3 (~4mm) and thundershowers on Days 6–7 (~10mm combined), skip your scheduled wheat irrigation this week — total expected rainfall (~14mm) covers the weekly crop water demand of ~25mm for wheat at tillering stage. Resume irrigation only if the thundershowers on Day 7 do not materialise. Check soil moisture at 15cm depth on Day 4 before deciding.

metadata.note: Synthetic stand-in — replace with real GDB/IMD export before production use

input: When will the monsoon arrive in coastal Karnataka this year and how should I prepare my paddy fields?
expected_output: IMD long-range forecast indicates monsoon onset over coastal Karnataka (Dakshina Kannada, Udupi, Uttara Kannada) around June 5–8, with a model error margin of ±4 days. Expected seasonal rainfall: 95–105% of LPA (Long Period Average) for the coastal belt. Pre-monsoon preparedness for paddy: (1) Complete nursery sowing 18–22 days before expected onset so seedlings are 25–30 days old at transplanting. (2) Repair bunds and level fields to retain 5–7cm of standing water. (3) Apply basal FYM @ 8–10 tonnes per hectare 7 days before transplanting. (4) Pre-monsoon ploughing exposes soil-borne pests and aids weed control. (5) Ensure drainage channels are clear to handle heavy spells (>65mm/day) that coastal Karnataka typically receives in July. Keep a 7-day buffer for onset delay — do not transplant before June 10 even if early rain arrives.

input: What are the current tomato prices in Kolar mandi and is it a good time to sell my harvest?
expected_output: Kolar APMC mandi tomato prices (last 3 days): Day 1 — ₹1,450 per quintal (modal), arrivals 2,800 quintals. Day 2 — ₹1,620 per quintal, arrivals 3,100 quintals. Day 3 — ₹1,780 per quintal, arrivals 2,400 quintals. 7-day average: ₹1,540 per quintal. Bangalore Kolar district supply is moderate; demand from Tamil Nadu and Kerala is firm. Selling recommendation: Prices are trending up (₹1,450 → ₹1,780, +22% over 3 days) and arrivals are not surging, suggesting tight supply. If your harvest is ready and field holding cost is low, sell within the next 2–3 days. If you can hold for 4–5 days, watch the daily modal price — if it crosses ₹1,900/quintal, sell immediately; if it drops below ₹1,600, sell to avoid further decline. Avoid holding beyond 7 days as prices typically correct once arrivals from new clusters begin.

input: What is the current MSP for wheat and should I sell to government procurement or private traders in Punjab?
expected_output: Current wheat MSP (Rabi marketing season 2026–27): ₹2,425 per quintal — an increase of ₹150 over the previous season. Government procurement at MSP via FCI and state agencies (PUNSUP, MARKFED in Punjab) is active at all mandis from April 1 to May 31. Private trader prices in Punjab mandis are currently ₹2,280–2,360 per quintal — below MSP. Selling recommendation: Sell to government procurement at MSP (₹2,425/quintal). The MSP is ₹65–145 per quintal above private trade, so the choice is clear unless your holding cost is high or your produce has high moisture (>14%) which may be rejected at procurement. Required quality: moisture ≤14%, foreign matter ≤2%, shriveled/broken grains ≤6%. Clean and dry your produce before taking it to the procurement centre. Bring Aadhaar-linked bank account for direct benefit transfer within 48 hours.

input: My cotton plants in Gujarat are showing yellowing between the veins on upper leaves. What soil nutrient am I likely deficient in?
expected_output: Interveinal chlorosis (yellowing between veins) on upper/younger leaves of cotton in Gujarat is a classic symptom of micronutrient deficiency. Differential diagnosis: (1) Iron (Fe) deficiency — most likely on calcareous/black cotton soils of Gujarat where pH is >7.8. Fe is unavailable at high pH. Confirm with DTPA-extractable Fe test (<4.5 ppm = deficient). (2) Manganese (Mn) deficiency — similar pattern but veins stay greener; common in light-textured soils. (3) Zinc (Zn) deficiency — usually appears as 'little leaf' with interveinal chlorosis; common in black cotton soils. Action: Get a soil test done at the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra or district soil testing lab. Quick corrective spray: 0.5% FeSO4 spray for iron deficiency, or 0.5% ZnSO4 spray for zinc deficiency. For long-term correction, apply ZnSO4 @ 25 kg/ha as basal. Do NOT apply lime or wood ash — these raise pH further and worsen the deficiency.

metadata.note: Synthetic stand-in — replace with real GDB/Soil Health Card export before production use

input: How do I test and manage soil salinity in my paddy field in Haryana?
expected_output: Soil salinity in Haryana paddy fields is increasingly common in Karnal, Kaithal, and Kurukshetra districts due to rising water table and poor-quality irrigation water. Diagnosis: (1) Collect a composite soil sample (10–15 sub-samples from 0–15cm depth) and test for Electrical Conductivity (EC) and pH. EC > 4 dS/m = saline soil; pH > 8.5 = sodic soil; both may co-occur. (2) Test irrigation water EC — if > 2 dS/m, it is contributing to salinity buildup. Management: (1) Leach salts by flooding the field with 10–15cm of good-quality water and allowing it to drain — repeat 2–3 times before transplanting. (2) Apply gypsum @ 5–10 tonnes per hectare if soil is sodic (pH > 8.5) — calcium replaces sodium. (3) Grow salt-tolerant varieties: CSR 30, CSR 36, CSR 43 (basmati) for sodic soils. (4) Improve drainage by installing subsurface drains at 30m spacing if water table is within 1.5m of surface. (5) Add organic matter (FYM @ 10 t/ha) to improve soil structure and reduce salt effect on roots. (6) Avoid rice-rice continuous cropping — rotate with dhaincha (Sesbania) green manure crop for one season to break the salinity cycle.

input: Good morning. What kinds of questions can you answer for me?
expected_output: Good morning. I can answer questions on 6 topics: (1) Crop cultivation — sowing, irrigation, fertilizer schedules for specific crops in specific states. (2) Plant protection — identifying diseases and pests, recommending treatment chemicals with dosages. (3) Weather — short-range forecasts and irrigation recommendations. (4) Market prices — current modal prices at APMC mandis and selling advice. (5) Soil health — testing and remediation for nutrient deficiencies and salinity. (6) Government schemes — eligibility, benefits, and application process for farmer welfare schemes. Ask your question with your crop name, state, and district if relevant, and I'll give the best answer I can.

input: I am a small farmer in Uttar Pradesh with 2 hectares. Am I eligible for PM-Kisan and how do I apply?
expected_output: PM-Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-Kisan) eligibility: Small and marginal farmers with cultivable land up to 2 hectares are eligible. Uttar Pradesh is a covered state. Benefits: Rs. 6,000 per year in three equal instalments of Rs. 2,000 each, credited directly to your bank account via DBT. Application process: (1) Visit your nearest Common Service Centre (CSC) or the official PM-Kisan portal at pmkisan.gov.in. (2) Carry your Aadhaar card, land records (khata/khasra), and bank account passbook. (3) The CSC operator will register you and verify your details. (4) Your first instalment typically arrives within 3 months of approval. (5) You can check your status at pmkisan.gov.in using your Aadhaar number. Note: PM-Kisan is income-support, not crop-specific — you continue farming as usual and the money is direct support.

metadata.note: Synthetic stand-in — replace with real schemes export (PM-Kisan FAQ from official portal). No crop facet because PM-Kisan is income support, not crop-specific.

input: How do I enrol my cotton crop in PM Fasal Bima Yojana in Gujarat this kharif season?
expected_output: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) for cotton in Gujarat — kharif 2026: (1) Eligibility: all cotton farmers in Gujarat, including sharecroppers and tenant farmers; Loanee farmers are enrolled automatically through their bank, non-loanee farmers can opt-in. (2) Premium: 1.5% of sum insured for cotton (the farmer's share; rest is subsidised by central and state government). For most Gujarat cotton growers this works out to roughly Rs. 1,200-1,800 per hectare depending on the insured amount. (3) Last date for kharif enrolment: typically July 31; contact your nearest bank branch or insurance company before sowing completes. (4) Required documents: Aadhaar, bank passbook, land records or sowing certificate, and crop sowing declaration. (5) Claims: filed automatically through the company based on area-yield data; payouts come within 2 months of harvest. (6) Helpline: 14447 or pmfby.gov.in.

metadata.note: Synthetic stand-in — replace with real PMFBY FAQ. cotton is the crop facet, PMFBY is the treatment/scheme facet.

input: What is the best treatment for tomato blight in Karnataka?
expected_output: For tomato blight (Phytophthora infestans) management in Karnataka: (1) Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb @ 2.5g per litre of water at 10-day intervals. (2) Remove and destroy all infected plant debris immediately after harvest. (3) Ensure proper plant spacing (60cm x 30cm) to improve air circulation and reduce humidity around foliage. (4) Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip irrigation instead. (5) As a preventive biocontrol, apply Trichoderma viride @ 5g per litre before monsoon. For severe outbreaks, use a combination spray: Metalaxyl-based fungicide followed by a protective copper-based spray 7 days later. Begin spray program before monsoon onset for best results.

input: How do I control thrips and whitefly on chilli crops in Andhra Pradesh?
expected_output: Integrated pest management for thrips and whitefly on chilli in Andhra Pradesh: (1) Install yellow sticky traps @ 50 per hectare to monitor and reduce adult populations. (2) Remove and destroy crop residues after harvest to eliminate alternate hosts. (3) Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL @ 0.5 ml per litre of water as a soil drench or seed treatment before sowing. (4) For spray application, use Spinosad 48% SC @ 0.3 ml per litre — effective against thrips with minimal impact on beneficial insects. (5) Release Trichogramma chilonis @ 50,000 eggs per hectare as a biological control agent. (6) Avoid consecutive sprays of the same mode-of-action group to prevent resistance development. Begin monitoring from seedling stage and continue through fruit development.
